title: #eng-platform: 1746211302
preview:
Thomas Baird: Flagging a potential footgun for Private: current hosted metrics include labels like `model`, `route`, and sometimes `tenant_id` (even if hashed). In a customer Prometheus that’s a fast path to TSDB pain. We need to decide what labels are allowed for the min pack.

Allison Grant: +1. This is exactly what we were trying to prevent with the Private standards. For Private we need bounded cardinality at the source, not “hope Prom handles it”. What’s the current worst offender?

Nadia Rahman: From the metering side we emit `redwood_requests_total{model=...,route=...,tenant=...}` (tenant is a short hash). In hosted it’s fine bc we shard and have remote write + big TSDB, but a 3-node on-prem cluster will melt.

Logan Wright: Also note: even without tenant, `route` * x `model` explodes. Customers tend to have a lot of routes and experiment with many model variants.

Dylan Brooks: For dashboards, the wireframes assumed a model dropdown. If we remove model label from everything, how do we still let customers slice by model?

Thomas Baird: We can still support it, just not as an unbounded raw label across all metrics. Proposal:
- Raw “golden signal” metrics only allow low-card labels: `service`, `namespace`, `pod` (optionally), `status_class` (2xx/4xx/5xx), `method` (GET/POST), maybe `deployment_mode`.
- Anything model/route/tenant goes into either (a) logs, (b) traces, or (c) *bounded* aggregates via recording rules.

Rafael Mendes: From SRE side: for Private we need the dashboards to render reliably at day 2, and alerts to not fall over due to query cost. I’m good with losing some slicing if it keeps Prom stable.

Aisha Bello: +1. Alerts should be on aggregates anyway (SLO burn, global error rates), not per-model. Per-model paging becomes noise.

Ethan Park: Control plane has similar issue: we tag a bunch of internal request metrics with `route` for the policy engine. In Private we can switch to `route_group` or `policy_id`? But even `policy_id` can be unbounded.

Allison Grant: The standards direction is: prefer “class” labels over identifiers. So `route_group` (e.g., `chat`, `embeddings`, `rerank`, `admin`) is ok. `route=/v1/chat/completions` is already borderline, but still bounded-ish. `tenant_id` is a hard no for metrics.

Sergio Costa: For the dashboards we can do:
- Overview panels: no model slice.
- Optional “Top N models” panel based on logs or on a recording rule that keeps only the top N.
But Prom can’t easily compute “top N labels” without scanning the full set… so we have to enforce the N at instrumentation or via relabeling.

Thomas Baird: Exactly. “topk()” still has to look at all series. If we already created 50k series, damage done.

Chloe Martin: Packaging-wise, Grafana dashboards can have variables that are optional. We could ship a dashboard that *tries* to populate model list only if `redwood_model_requests_total` exists, otherwise hides panels.

Nadia Rahman: What about making `model` available only on a single, intentionally scoped metric? Like `redwood_model_requests_total` with a hard cap and a default aggregation bucket.

Logan Wright: I like that. Similar to how some systems do `*_by_model` metrics. We can gate/limit:
- Keep `model_family` (e.g., `llama`, `mistral`) or `model_alias` from config, not the full registry id.
- Cap to N configured models (N=20?) and everything else goes into `model="other"`.

Dylan Brooks: That works for the runtime dashboard. Most customers have a small number of “real” models anyway; experiments can fall into other.

Thomas Baird: Concrete plan (please poke holes):
1) Remove `tenant_id` label from ALL Prom metrics in Private build. Keep tenant context in logs only (hashed) and in audit logs as appropriate.
2) Replace `route` label with `route_group` everywhere we can. For the gateway we can maintain `route_group` mapping in config.
3) For model visibility:
   - Default metrics: no `model` label.
   - Add ONE metric family `redwood_model_requests_total{model_alias=...}` and `redwood_model_request_errors_total{model_alias=...,status_class=...}` with a strict allowlist + cap.
   - Anything not in allowlist is `model_alias="other"`.
4) Provide recording rules for dashboards/alerts that roll up to per-service + global.
5) Document an *explicit* opt-in for higher cardinality (with scary warning) for customers who really want per-model/per-route metrics.

Allison Grant: On (5) I’d prefer we don’t even provide “turn on high-card” in v1. Support will get dragged into Prom sizing and query timeouts. Maybe we provide “advanced” as “unsupported / best effort” with a big disclaimer.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. If it’s a Helm toggle, customers will flip it and then blame us. If we must have it, hide behind `observability.experimentalHighCardinality=true` and default false.

Ethan Park: For control plane, `policy_id` is also a no. We can use `decision=allow|deny` and `reason_class` maybe. For debugging specific policies, logs.

Sergio Costa: Can we do Prom relabeling in the chart to drop accidental labels if someone reintroduces them?

Thomas Baird: Yes, but relabeling happens at scrape time; the target still exports those series, Prom will drop labels/series as configured. It’s a safety net, not the primary solution.

Logan Wright: We can ship a default scrape config snippet for the Redwood jobs that drops known-bad labels if they exist (`tenant_id`, `request_id`, etc.). Example:
```yaml
metric_relabel_configs:
  - source_labels: [tenant_id]
    regex: .+
    action: labeldrop
  - source_labels: [request_id]
    regex: .+
    action: labeldrop
```
But +1 we should fix at instrumentation first.

Nadia Rahman: Confirming we do NOT currently export `request_id` as a metric label (only log field), but I’ve seen some teams propose it. Let’s explicitly call it out as forbidden.

Allison Grant: Decision-wise, I’ll update the Private metrics & alerting standards doc with:
- Forbidden labels: `tenant_id`, `user_id`, `request_id`, `prompt_hash`, raw `route`, raw `model` (except allowlisted `model_alias`).
- Bounded allowed labels list.
- Naming conventions: `redwood_private_*` vs `redwood_*`? (I think keep `redwood_*` but enforce semantics by build/install mode.)

Dylan Brooks: For dashboards: I’ll adjust runtime overview to use `model_alias` panels only when present, and keep everything else aggregated. Also will avoid dashboard variables that query `label_values(model)` unless we’re sure the label is bounded.

Chloe Martin: +1 please don’t use `label_values()` on high-card metrics in Private dashboards. It can be brutal. Use a static custom variable from a JSON list if needed.

Aisha Bello: Alerting: we should ensure SLO burn alerts use recording rules that don’t include model/route labels. It should be something like `service="gateway"` and global.

Rafael Mendes: One more ask: can alert annotations include a hint like “if you need per-model debug, go to logs view and filter by model_alias / request_id”? Oncall will appreciate that.

Sergio Costa: Yep. I’ll add runbook notes to alert annotations.

Thomas Baird: Sounds aligned. Summarizing agreed direction:
- No tenant label in metrics.
- route -> route_group.
- model only via bounded `model_alias` allowlist + `other` bucket.
- dashboards/alerts query aggregates; model panels optional.
- optional relabel safety net in Helm, but primary fix is instrumentation.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: I’ll capture this as an addendum in the standards + point ENG-19143 at this thread.

Logan Wright: I’ll take action item to add a metric review gate in CI for Private builds (basic cardinality/lint: fail if forbidden labels appear).

Nadia Rahman: I’ll open a small PR to switch runtime counters over to `route_group` and introduce `model_alias` mapping (from config) with cap.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll update the dashboard wireframes and note the “bounded model list” assumption.

Rafael Mendes: +1. This will save us a lot of pain during air-gapped validations.

Thomas Baird: Great. If anyone sees a metric with `model` or `tenant` in private builds after next week, please @ me and I’ll block the release.

Allison Grant: (Also: we should make sure docs call out that per-tenant analytics are not supported in Private Prom metrics. Customers can do that in their SIEM/logs if they want.)

Sergio Costa: ack. Will add a note in the dashboard catalog + the runbook.

Chloe Martin: :eyes: if you need help making Grafana panels conditionally hide when metric absent, ping me.

Thomas Baird: Will do. Thanks all.

title: #eng-platform: 1746124805
preview:
Dylan Brooks: Kicking off scope decision for Private Observability Minimum Pack dashboards. Need clear must-have vs nice-to-have for v1 so packaging + QA can lock.

Dylan Brooks: Current wireframes (WIP) here: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/priv-obs-min-pack-dashboard-wireframes (see “Runtime Overview” + “Control Plane Health”).

Dylan Brooks: Please reply with (a) MUST ship in v1, (b) NICE-to-have (v1.1+), and owner. Also any panels that are “don’t ship (cardinality / too brittle)”.

Sergio Costa: For v1, I’d strongly push these as MUST:
1) Runtime Overview (golden signals + throughput) — panels: request RPS, p95/p99 latency, error rate, tokens/sec, queue depth, batch size, GPU util, GPU mem, OOM/restarts.
2) Control Plane Health — API gateway, policy/router, auth integration errors, config sync, model registry connectivity.
3) Prometheus/Grafana “Is it working” — scrape health, target down count, TSDB head series, disk fill, remote_write failures (if enabled).

Sergio Costa: NICE:
- KV cache deep dive (hit rate, evictions, prefix cache) — useful but can confuse customers early.
- Per-model comparison dashboard (requires model label hygiene; risky for Private).

Chloe Martin: +1 to “Is it working” / scrape health as MUST. Support tickets always start with “dashboards empty.” We should have a dashboard that answers: datasource connected? Prom scraping? are Redwood targets up?

Chloe Martin: Also: if we’re packaging dashboards via ConfigMaps, MUST include a “Dashboard provisioning status” note in the dashboard description (and a link to runbook). Otherwise customers will assume Grafana is broken.

Allison Grant: From standards side: MUST include dashboards that map to golden signals + top failure modes we’ve seen: latency/error/saturation + restart loops + storage pressure.

Allison Grant: I’d classify MUST as:
- Inference Runtime Overview (golden signals)
- GPU / Resource Pressure (can be part of runtime overview if not too big)
- Control Plane Health
- Observability Stack Health (Prom scrape + Grafana datasource + disk)

Allison Grant: NICE:
- Multi-cluster rollup views (out of min pack)
- Deep per-route breakdowns (cardinality + privacy)

Ethan Park: Control plane view MUST includes:
- API gateway 5xx/4xx, upstream timeouts, auth failures
- router/policy decision errors + fallback counts
- config reconciler loop duration + failures
- model registry: sync age, last successful sync, error rate

Ethan Park: NICE:
- “Policy changes audit” panels (probably belongs to audit logging doc vs dashboard)

Rafael Mendes: From SRE/oncall: MUST dashboards should let you debug the pagers we’re adding.

Rafael Mendes: For alert coverage we’re proposing (latency burn + availability + GPU OOM + restart loops), we need a clear click-path in dashboards:
- start at Runtime Overview
- jump links to GPU / Pods / K8s events
- jump links to Control Plane

Rafael Mendes: If we can’t do “jump links” in Grafana easily with packaged dashboards, then at least text panels at top with runbook links.

Nadia Rahman: Caution on per-route/per-model panels. Private has much more variance, and we’ve been trying to cap labels. If the dashboard depends on `route` or `model` label existing on every metric, it’ll break once we aggregate.

Nadia Rahman: Suggest MUST panels use recording rules like:
- `redwood:request_rate:sum`
- `redwood:error_rate:sum`
- `redwood:latency_seconds:p95`

Nadia Rahman: NICE panels can use breakdown dimensions, but they should be behind a templated variable w/ defaults and be clear it may be empty if the customer disables those labels.

Logan Wright: Agree. For v1 packaging we should assume bounded cardinality and prefer “service-level” views.

Logan Wright: For logs: are we doing a dashboard for log pipeline health (collector buffer, dropped logs, retries)? If yes, I think MUST. Otherwise we’ll get “Splunk exporter broken” and no baseline visibility.

Dylan Brooks: Good call. I had “Log pipeline health” as a separate dash but wasn’t sure if it’s in v1. Sounds like MUST if we’re shipping a default collector.

Vivek Kulkarni: Product POV: minimum pack should feel complete for day-2 ops without needing bespoke setup. If logs are in scope for v1, there needs to be a basic dashboard showing ingestion/forwarding health + drop rate.

Vivek Kulkarni: Also: keep count low. If we ship 12 dashboards we’ll lose the “minimum supported” message. Target 4–6 dashboards max.

Sergio Costa: Proposal: v1 = 5 dashboards.
1) Redwood Private - Runtime Overview (owner: Omar for metrics, I can assemble)
2) Redwood Private - GPU & Resource Pressure (owner: Sergio)
3) Redwood Private - Control Plane Health (owner: Ethan + Sergio)
4) Redwood Private - Observability Stack Health (owner: Chloe)
5) Redwood Private - Log Pipeline Health (owner: Logan / Telemetry, with help from Sergio)

Sergio Costa: NICE backlog:
- KV cache deep dive
- Model registry deep dive
- Upgrade/installer dashboard (preflight/postinstall results)
- Per-model/per-route breakdown

Chloe Martin: :thumbsup: on 5 dashboards. For “Observability Stack Health” I’ll include panels:
- Prometheus targets up (by job)
- scrape duration + samples scraped
- tsdb head series + WAL replay time
- PVC usage (prom + grafana)
- grafana datasource status (if we can query it; otherwise add a checklist text panel)

Allison Grant: Works. Please ensure dashboard names include “Private” and don’t reuse Hosted names; Support gets confused when screenshots differ.

Rafael Mendes: For MUST: add runbook links in every dashboard header.
Example text panel:
- “If panels are empty: check Prometheus targets => runbook https://confluence/redwood/private-obs-day2#scrape”

Rafael Mendes: Also please avoid “per-pod” as the default view; it’s noisy. Start aggregated, allow drilldown.

Nadia Rahman: One more constraint: queries should use recording rules when possible. Direct histograms across high-card labels will melt customer Prom.

Nadia Rahman: If you need latency, use the SLO/recording series, not raw `*_bucket`.

Dylan Brooks: Ok, I’ll write this up as the scope decision:
MUST (v1): Runtime Overview, GPU/Resource Pressure, Control Plane Health, Observability Stack Health, Log Pipeline Health.
NICE (v1.1+): KV cache, per-model/per-route, registry deep dive, installer/upgrade.

Dylan Brooks: Owners:
- Runtime Overview: Sergio (+ Omar for metrics)
- GPU/Resource Pressure: Sergio
- Control Plane Health: Ethan (+ Sergio)
- Observability Stack Health: Chloe
- Log Pipeline Health: Logan (+ Sergio)

Dylan Brooks: Quick links to the draft dashboards folder (where we’ll land JSON): https://github.com/redwood-inference/observability-pack/tree/main/dashboards/private-min-pack (will create today)

Sergio Costa: :eyes: on the repo folder. Once it exists I’ll push first cut of Runtime Overview + GPU dash. For runtime overview I’m basing panels on the wireframes doc section “Golden Signals + Throughput”.

Ethan Park: I’ll send Sergio the control plane metrics inventory (gateway/router/config/model-registry) later today. Some of these are in flux pending the label normalization PR.

Logan Wright: For log pipeline dash, I’ll draft collector metrics list (buffer fill %, dropped records, retry count, sink errors). We need to confirm which collector we’re standardizing on for v1.

Chloe Martin: Please add an explicit “Grafana provisioning” panel w/ instructions. Support keeps hitting the case where dashboards don’t load because folder permissions or sidecar label missing.

Allison Grant: :+1: please make sure these follow the Private metrics/label standards (no tenant_id, no raw route as label). If you need multi-tenant visibility, use hashed/aggregated where allowed.

Dylan Brooks: Noted. I’ll also add to the scope doc: “No dashboard panels that require `tenant_id`, raw `request_id`, or unbounded `model` label.”

Dylan Brooks: I’ll drop a short decision summary in the PR description when we create the dashboard bundle PR.

Sergio Costa: Sounds good. Once the folder is live, tag me on the PR and I’ll start wiring query expressions to recording rules.

Rafael Mendes: Can we also include a “Top alerts firing” panel somewhere? It helps validate alert->dashboard loop.

Sergio Costa: That’s NICE IMO unless we have alertmanager datasource prewired in every private install. Might be brittle.

Chloe Martin: Agree, unless we standardize alertmanager integration. We can add a text panel pointing to “Alerting” page in Grafana.

Dylan Brooks: Let’s keep it out for v1. Add as NICE and revisit once installer prewires alertmanager.

Dylan Brooks: Locking scope as above unless objections by EOD. :lock: (not literally)

Vivek Kulkarni: No objections. Please keep dashboard count + naming consistent with “minimum supported” messaging.

Dylan Brooks: Cool. I’ll post the summary to the thread in #eng-releases once the repo path exists + we have owners confirmed.

Logan Wright: For logs: are we doing a dashboard for log pipeline health (collector buffer, dropped logs, retries)? If yes, I think MUST. Otherwise we’ll get “Splunk exporter broken” and no baseline visibility.

Dylan Brooks: Good call. I had “Log pipeline health” as a separate dash but wasn’t sure if it’s in v1. Sounds like MUST if we’re shipping a default collector.

Vivek Kulkarni: Product POV: minimum pack should feel complete for day-2 ops without needing bespoke setup. If logs are in scope for v1, there needs to be a basic dashboard showing ingestion/forwarding health + drop rate.

Vivek Kulkarni: Also: keep count low. If we ship 12 dashboards we’ll lose the “minimum supported” message. Target 4–6 dashboards max.

Sergio Costa: Proposal: v1 = 5 dashboards.
1) Redwood Private - Runtime Overview (owner: Omar for metrics, I can assemble)
2) Redwood Private - GPU & Resource Pressure (owner: Sergio)
3) Redwood Private - Control Plane Health (owner: Ethan + Sergio)
4) Redwood Private - Observability Stack Health (owner: Chloe)
5) Redwood Private - Log Pipeline Health (owner: Logan / Telemetry, with help from Sergio)

Sergio Costa: NICE backlog:
- KV cache deep dive
- Model registry deep dive
- Upgrade/installer dashboard (preflight/postinstall results)
- Per-model/per-route breakdown

Chloe Martin: :thumbsup: on 5 dashboards. For “Observability Stack Health” I’ll include panels:
- Prometheus targets up (by job)
- scrape duration + samples scraped
- tsdb head series + WAL replay time
- PVC usage (prom + grafana)
- grafana datasource status (if we can query it; otherwise add a checklist text panel)

Allison Grant: Works. Please ensure dashboard names include “Private” and don’t reuse Hosted names; Support gets confused when screenshots differ.

Rafael Mendes: For MUST: add runbook links in every dashboard header.
Example text panel:
- “If panels are empty: check Prometheus targets => runbook https://confluence/redwood/private-obs-day2#scrape”

Rafael Mendes: Also please avoid “per-pod” as the default view; it’s noisy. Start aggregated, allow drilldown.

Nadia Rahman: One more constraint: queries should use recording rules when possible. Direct histograms across high-card labels will melt customer Prom.

Nadia Rahman: If you need latency, use the SLO/recording series, not raw `*_bucket`.

Dylan Brooks: Ok, I’ll write this up as the scope decision:
MUST (v1): Runtime Overview, GPU/Resource Pressure, Control Plane Health, Observability Stack Health, Log Pipeline Health.
NICE (v1.1+): KV cache, per-model/per-route, registry deep dive, installer/upgrade.

Dylan Brooks: Owners:
- Runtime Overview: Sergio (+ Omar for metrics)
- GPU/Resource Pressure: Sergio
- Control Plane Health: Ethan (+ Sergio)
- Observability Stack Health: Chloe
- Log Pipeline Health: Logan (+ Sergio)

Dylan Brooks: Quick links to the draft dashboards folder (where we’ll land JSON): https://github.com/redwood-inference/observability-pack/tree/main/dashboards/private-min-pack (will create today)

Sergio Costa: :eyes: on the repo folder. Once it exists I’ll push first cut of Runtime Overview + GPU dash. For runtime overview I’m basing panels on the wireframes doc section “Golden Signals + Throughput”.

Ethan Park: I’ll send Sergio the control plane metrics inventory (gateway/router/config/model-registry) later today. Some of these are in flux pending the label normalization PR.

Logan Wright: For log pipeline dash, I’ll draft collector metrics list (buffer fill %, dropped records, retry count, sink errors). We need to confirm which collector we’re standardizing on for v1.

Chloe Martin: Please add an explicit “Grafana provisioning” panel w/ instructions. Support keeps hitting the case where dashboards don’t load because folder permissions or sidecar label missing.

Allison Grant: :+1: please make sure these follow the Private metrics/label standards (no tenant_id, no raw route as label). If you need multi-tenant visibility, use hashed/aggregated where allowed.

Dylan Brooks: Noted. I’ll also add to the scope doc: “No dashboard panels that require `tenant_id`, raw `request_id`, or unbounded `model` label.”

Dylan Brooks: I’ll drop a short decision summary in the PR description when we create the dashboard bundle PR.

Sergio Costa: Sounds good. Once the folder is live, tag me on the PR and I’ll start wiring query expressions to recording rules.

Rafael Mendes: Can we also include a “Top alerts firing” panel somewhere? It helps validate alert->dashboard loop.

Sergio Costa: That’s NICE IMO unless we have alertmanager datasource prewired in every private install. Might be brittle.

Chloe Martin: Agree, unless we standardize alertmanager integration. We can add a text panel pointing to “Alerting” page in Grafana.

Dylan Brooks: Let’s keep it out for v1. Add as NICE and revisit once installer prewires alertmanager.

Dylan Brooks: Locking scope as above unless objections by EOD. :lock: (not literally)

Vivek Kulkarni: No objections. Please keep dashboard count + naming consistent with “minimum supported” messaging.

Dylan Brooks: Cool. I’ll post the summary to the thread in #eng-releases once the repo path exists + we have owners confirmed.

title: #eng-platform: 1745558642
preview:
Ben Carter: FYI seeing a weird spike in invoice-preview tokens for tenant `org-472` after yesterday's routing changes :thinking_face:
Ben Carter: looks like a lot of tokens are landing in the 'unattributed' bucket then getting reclassified later 
Jess Lin: ugh, is this the fallback->shadow path again? :eyes: any request_id samples?
Ben Carter: grabbed a few, here's one: req=01F8XQ9Z... route=primary -> fallback -> shadow -> meter.ended
Liam O'Neal: that chain rings a bell. earlier change to sessionization boundaries moved end-of-session attribution to the final consumer which means late-arriving kv-evictions can reassign credits 
Nayla R.: can we get the event ordering / ingestion window around those reqs? I'm worried the aggregator windowing isn't stable across retries
Ben Carter: query I ran (simplified):
```
SELECT req_id, route_path, event_time, token_count, attribution_state
FROM metering.events
WHERE tenant_id='org-472' AND event_time BETWEEN '2025-06-01' AND '2025-06-02'
ORDER BY req_id, event_time;
```
Ben Carter: results show duplicate token_count rows where a follow-up evt with attribution=unattributed arrives ~10-30s later and overrides earlier attribution in the preview
Sam: so invoice-preview (fast path) uses first-seen attribution but final ledger (slow path) dedupes/merges? that mismatch would explain preview spikes
Jess Lin: exactly. preview consumes streaming-agg snapshots, finalizer consumes join+dedupe. We changed the preview window to be more permissive last week for perf -> exposed this edge
Liam O'Neal: possible fixes: 1) tighten preview join window, 2) publish provisional attribution state to preview UI (mark as tentative), 3) add a short grace TTL for reassignments before preview tallies
Nayla R.: option 3 seems lowest risk to UX. implement a 60s 'soft lock' on attribution for preview only. if a later event attempts to reassigned within TTL, keep preview as-is but emit an anomaly signal
Jess Lin: agree. we should also add telemetry counts for 'reassign-within-ttl' so billing ops can triage tenants with noisy clients
metering-bot: alert created: MTR-REASSIGN-01 for org-472 (rule: reassignments > 5 in 5m). Incident link: https://ops.internal/alerts/MTR-REASSIGN-01
Ben Carter: can someone own a quick canary? I can ship a preview patch to add the TTL + anomaly counter to the streaming service
Sam: I can run a canary on shadow plane for a few tenants, will monitor invoice-preview dashboards and ledger diffs (use route=shadow tag)
Liam O'Neal: also add a per-request trace id in preview telemetry so we can link preview rows to finalizer rows. even just the req_id is enough
Jess Lin: ack. I'll update the console card to show a 'preview confidence' flag (green/yellow/red) based on reassign counts -> product wants not to surprise customers
Nayla R.: note for rollout: we need a migration plan in case TTL causes underreporting in short bursts. plan: enable soft TTL by default for 24h canary, compare total tokens over 24h vs baseline, then widen if stable
Ben Carter: + add a E2E test for replayed late events to prevent regressions. sample test: inject events out-of-order, assert preview delta < 1% after reconciliation
Sam: I'll run the canary today at 1600 PT. tagging @billing-ops on the alert and on the dashboard watchlist
Jess Lin: PR link once created: https://git.redwood/infer/streaming/pulls/2981 (will add unit tests + telemetry)
Ben Carter: thanks. if we see persistent reassign spikes after TTL, next step is to look at the fallback policy: maybe we should prefer last-known attribution from primary route rather than shadow overrides
Liam O'Neal: noted. also let's add a lightweight debug log line in the finalizer when attribution flips: `attribution_flip(req_id, from, to, delta_tokens)`
Nayla R.: and add a console card alert: 'Attribution flips > threshold' so support can proactively reach out to noisy SDK authors
metering-bot: canary deploy scheduled: streaming-preview-v2 -> shadow tenants (ids: org-472, org-512) at 2025-06-05T16:00Z
Ben Carter: cool. I'll follow up with a short postmortem thread if the canary shows regression. thanks all :thumbsup:

title: #eng-platform: 1741753119
preview:
Allison Grant: quick q on demo env alert routing before I flip the switches. should demo alerts land in #sales or #support?

Markus Klein: neither feels great tbh. #sales will ignore (too much noise during calls). #support will assume it’s customer-prod.

Chloe Martin: +1. also eng-platform folks will miss it if it’s in #sales.

Allison Grant: current config is pointing to #support bc it was the easiest existing integration. but agree it’s confusing.

Sean Gallagher: we should treat demo env as its own “prod-like” surface. recommendation:
- create #demo-env-alerts (or #demo-alerts)
- route alerts there
- have a user group / alias for oncall: @demo-oncall
- escalation: @sre-oncall as secondary for Sev2+ demo outages

Avery Johnson: from field side: if it hits #sales, it becomes a fire drill + 10 pings. i'd rather have a clean place + a single person to reach.

Markus Klein: yes please. SEs need a single “who’s got it” alias. if it’s just a channel, it still becomes "anyone around?".

Allison Grant: ok. for @demo-oncall, do we want SE rotation or SRE rotation? (or both?)

Sean Gallagher: primary should be SE rotation (Markus’s team), since most issues are "reset / seed / quota" and not infra. SRE secondary for platform-wide incidents.

Naomi Feldman: also +1 for separation. demo alerts should not land in #support since that channel gets screenshotted sometimes during customer escalations. keep demo routing internal + least-privilege membership.

Chloe Martin: naming suggestion: #gtm-demo-alerts? or #demo-env-alerts? i prefer #demo-env-alerts.

Markus Klein: #demo-env-alerts works. keep it obvious it’s environment-level, not "runbook chatter".

Allison Grant: cool. I’ll:
1) create #demo-env-alerts
2) define @demo-oncall user group (SE rotation) + add @sre-oncall as fallback
3) update alert routing rules to send demo env alerts to #demo-env-alerts and mention @demo-oncall on page-worthy alerts

Sean Gallagher: on paging: we should not page for every threshold breach. can we do:
- Slack only for warning / "dashboard freshness" type things
- page @demo-oncall only for hard failures: healthcheck fail, sustained 5xx spike, quota exhaustion, synthetic traffic job dead

Allison Grant: yup. that matches what I’ve got in the draft. will tune severities.

Redwood Alertbot: (config-check) Detected alert route "demo-env" currently posting to #support. Next deploy will apply changes.

Avery Johnson: can we also pin a short "what to do if you're in a live demo" message in #demo-env-alerts? like 3 bullet workaround steps + link to runbook.

Markus Klein: +1. we can paste the "backup mode" steps + the one-command healthcheck.

Allison Grant: done. once channel is up I’ll add a pinned message with:
- `democtl reset --project <x>`
- `democtl healthcheck`
- if dashboards empty: run synthetic traffic job preset
and link to reset runbook.

Sean Gallagher: decision captured: NO alerts to #sales or #support. route to #demo-env-alerts with @demo-oncall alias; SRE secondary.

Markus Klein: :thumbsup: thanks. this should reduce the "who owns this" confusion.

Allison Grant: I’ll ship the routing update today and post in #general once @demo-oncall is live.

title: #eng-platform: 1741667003
preview:
Mei Lin: quick implementation detail we need to lock today: where do we store demo seed definitions (org/projects/routes/prompt sets/etc)? JSON config in repo vs DB migrations (or something in control plane DB).

Devon Price: +1. bootstrap CLI in pr-28451 currently has seed objects inline in code. It’s getting gross. I’d rather load a config file so SEs can diff changes.

Chloe Martin: migrations feels wrong for “demo content”. migrations should be for schema + maybe default roles. demo seed is operational data.

Ethan Park: agree. also migrations run in places we don’t want (non-demo envs). we already have guardrails in reset CLI but I don’t want to couple demo content to DB lifecycle.

Dylan Brooks: what’s the requirement from Console side: stable IDs/names so dashboards always have the right filters + empty state isn’t weird.

Mei Lin: yep. we need predictable naming + stable route IDs for the synthetic traffic generator and for the “known-good” check.

Markus Klein: from SE perspective, config in repo is better. when something breaks in the field we can hotfix seed definitions and roll it with the next demo env deploy. migrations are slower + scarier.

Leila Farouk: if it’s config, we should validate it (schema) and add a CI test that `demo bootstrap --dry-run` passes. otherwise someone will typo a model slug and we find out during a live demo.

Devon Price: proposal:
- store seed definitions in `redwood` repo as JSON (or YAML) under `internal/demo/seed/seed-definitions.json`
- bootstrap/reset CLI reads that file, validates, then applies idempotent upserts via control plane API
- synthetic traffic job reads the same definitions (or consumes “exported IDs” written during bootstrap)

Chloe Martin: +1 JSON over YAML if we’re being pragmatic. less debate about formatting; can generate it too.

Dylan Brooks: does JSON let us do comments? (we’ll want notes like “this route is used in reliability demo”.)

Mei Lin: JSON doesn’t, but we can put a `notes` field as a string. or do JSONC but then we need a parser.

Ethan Park: please no JSONC. keep it boring.

Talia Benson: what’s the decision point? need to unblock Eng Platform + SE enablement.

Mei Lin: main question: config in repo vs DB migrations vs “store in DB table and edit via admin UI”.

Chloe Martin: admin UI is scope creep. also creates drift (what’s in UI vs what’s in code). we want predictable seeded env.

Devon Price: and “seed in DB” means you have to bootstrap the bootstrap. plus review/audit is worse.

Markus Klein: config file in repo is also easiest for me to understand + explain in runbook (“seed defs live here, changes go through PR”).

Leila Farouk: as long as we treat it like code. add:
- JSON schema validation
- `known-good` snapshot test (a diff of expected created resources)
- healthcheck verifies all referenced models exist + pinned versions resolve

Ethan Park: one more: we need environment guardrails so someone can’t point bootstrap at prod org. the config file should include an explicit `demo_org_slug` and CLI should refuse mismatches.

Devon Price: already have env guardrails in pr-28691. can extend: `--org` must match `seed-definitions.json` + must match allowlist.

Dylan Brooks: ok. for Console stability, please ensure we include:
- stable project slugs
- stable “routes” names
- a default time range + seeded traffic so dashboards look alive

Mei Lin: ack. I’ll add “required fields” in the schema.

Mei Lin: rough shape:
```json
{
  "version": "2026-03-11",
  "demo_org_slug": "redwood-demo",
  "projects": [
    {
      "slug": "core-api",
      "display_name": "Core API Demo",
      "notes": "Streaming + structured output + tool calling"
    },
    {
      "slug": "prod-econ",
      "display_name": "Production Economics",
      "notes": "Batching/caching toggles + cost breakdown"
    }
  ],
  "routes": [
    {
      "project_slug": "core-api",
      "route_slug": "chat-streaming",
      "model_pin": "rw-llama3.1-70b-instruct@2026-02-15",
      "fallback_policy": "family+region",
      "notes": "Used in live demo step 3"
    }
  ],
  "prompt_sets": [
    {
      "project_slug": "prod-econ",
      "name": "Support QA - known good",
      "notes": "Synthetic prompts only"
    }
  ]
}
```

Chloe Martin: looks good. keep `version` so we can talk about changes and rollbacks.

Ethan Park: +1. also pinning string should match whatever Mina/Ravi are implementing in the pin/fallback PR.

Markus Klein: decision for today: repo JSON config + CLI loader + schema validation. not migrations.

Talia Benson: capturing as decision:
- Seed definitions live as versioned JSON in repo
- CLI/bootstrap reads/validates + idempotent API upserts
- No DB migrations for demo content
Action items:
1) Mei to open PR moving inline seeds -> `seed-definitions.json` + add JSON schema
2) Devon to update bootstrap CLI to read config + emit created IDs for synthetic traffic
3) Leila to add CI dry-run + regression test

Mei Lin: +1, I’ll take (1). will tag @Devon Price / @Leila Farouk on the PR.

Devon Price: I’ll take (2). will also add `demo bootstrap --print-summary` so SEs can copy/paste into runbook.

Leila Farouk: I’ll take (3). want to also add a healthcheck assertion that “seed defs referenced model pins exist” so we catch catalog drift.

Dylan Brooks: thanks. ping me when the config stabilizes; I’ll align the Console walkthrough to the final project/route names.

Ethan Park: pls keep least-privilege in mind for any API keys used by the bootstrap job. (ENG-4855)

Mei Lin: yup. closing loop here — decision made.

Chloe Martin: looks good. keep `version` so we can talk about changes and rollbacks.

Ethan Park: +1. also pinning string should match whatever Mina/Ravi are implementing in the pin/fallback PR.

Markus Klein: decision for today: repo JSON config + CLI loader + schema validation. not migrations.

Talia Benson: capturing as decision:
- Seed definitions live as versioned JSON in repo
- CLI/bootstrap reads/validates + idempotent API upserts
- No DB migrations for demo content
Action items:
1) Mei to open PR moving inline seeds -> `seed-definitions.json` + add JSON schema
2) Devon to update bootstrap CLI to read config + emit created IDs for synthetic traffic
3) Leila to add CI dry-run + regression test

Mei Lin: +1, I’ll take (1). will tag @Devon Price / @Leila Farouk on the PR.

Devon Price: I’ll take (2). will also add `demo bootstrap --print-summary` so SEs can copy/paste into runbook.

Leila Farouk: I’ll take (3). want to also add a healthcheck assertion that “seed defs referenced model pins exist” so we catch catalog drift.

Dylan Brooks: thanks. ping me when the config stabilizes; I’ll align the Console walkthrough to the final project/route names.

Ethan Park: pls keep least-privilege in mind for any API keys used by the bootstrap job. (ENG-4855)

Mei Lin: yup. closing loop here — decision made.

title: #eng-platform: 1741163330
preview:
Yara Haddad: dropping early backtest results for the cache hit-rate rec templates. pulled 14 days of telemetry aggregates (hosted + a couple dedicated tenants we have consent for internal analysis) and ran the heuristics against “known bad” windows from incident notes + SUP tickets.

Yara Haddad: tldr: 3 templates look safe-ish to ship in MVP behind guardrails, 2 are too noisy.

Yara Haddad: doc w/ tables + queries: https://drive.redwoodinference.com/eng-platform/cache-recs/backtest_v0_3 (sorry, still messy)

Hassan Mirza: nice. can you paste the headline numbers here? esp precision on the ones you think are “ship”.

Yara Haddad: yep. for each template i measured: (a) precision = % of fired recs that correlate w/ hit-rate dip + cost delta in same window, (b) “actionability” = did it point to a knob we actually expose today, (c) false positive drivers.

Yara Haddad: 1) TEMPLATE: “Enable caching on route” (detects `policy_disabled` misses > X% AND route has cacheable=true flag off)
- precision: 0.86 (31/36)
- common FP: routes that are intentionally uncached due to privacy policy, but those are tagged “no_cache_reason=privacy” in gateway (only present for ~60% rn)
- guardrail: only fire if route label allowlisted + no_cache_reason != privacy

Yara Haddad: 2) TEMPLATE: “Increase `max_prefix_cache_bytes` / reduce eviction pressure” (eviction_rate high + prefix_hit_rate down + wasted_prefill up)
- precision: 0.74 (23/31)
- FP: workload shifts to longer prompts (prompt_len_bucket moves) = looks like eviction pressure but is just new shape
- guardrail: require stable prompt_len distribution (JS-divergence < 0.15) OR include copy that says “workload shape changed; validate first”

Yara Haddad: 3) TEMPLATE: “Prompt canonicalization” (hash_mismatch misses high + high prompt similarity proxy)
- precision: 0.69 (18/26)
- FP: model version mismatch masquerading as hash_mismatch in 2 cases (older runtime bug fixed in pr-18430/18431)
- guardrail: only enable once new metric names + version_mismatch reason fully rolled out in all regions

Yara Haddad: 4) TEMPLATE: “Increase TTL” (ttl_expired misses high)
- precision: 0.41 (9/22)
- FP: chat workloads where reuse is multi-turn but gaps are longer than TTL; the rec says “increase TTL” but then cache memory blows up and overall latency regresses. also TTL_expired is currently undercounted in streaming retry paths in one region.
- my vote: do NOT ship in MVP

Yara Haddad: 5) TEMPLATE: “Routing locality (pin to region / reduce cross-region)” (region_mismatch / low locality proxy)
- precision: 0.33 (5/15)
- FP: most of these are expected multi-region failover events. SRE already sees them; telling customers “pin to region” is risky.
- my vote: internal-only note for now

Logan Wright: this is super helpful. on (1) — can we make “no_cache_reason” mandatory in gateway tagging? would kill a bunch of FPs.

Ethan Park: we can, but it’s a migration. today routes created pre-jan don’t have it. we can default to “unspecified” and only fire if present != privacy, like Yara said.

Nadia Rahman: +1 on being conservative. for MVP, i’d rather under-fire than spam support.

Aditya Rao: from product POV: i’m ok shipping only 2-3 templates if we’re confident. we need “actionable and safe” more than breadth.

Paula Mendes: question: for (2) “increase max_prefix_cache_bytes” — are we sure that knob is available across Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private? i don’t want recs that only apply to dedicated, then show up in hosted.

Mei Lin: knob exposure matrix:
- Hosted: not customer-configurable directly; but we can surface as “contact support / Redwood can adjust”
- Dedicated: customer can set via route config (depending on contract tier)
- Private: customer controls config, but we need exact flag name
We should template the copy by deployment_mode.

Hassan Mirza: agree. also on (2) — can we instead recommend “reduce prompt_len max / enforce truncation” when the prompt bucket shift is the driver? less risky than allocating more cache.

Yara Haddad: i tried a “prompt_len shift” secondary branch. it worked in a couple windows but hard to make it not sound accusatory (“your prompts got longer”). maybe: “workload prompt length increased; cache may be less effective. consider bucketing routes or prompt truncation where acceptable.”

Miguel Santos: from CS perspective: (1) and (3) are the ones customers actually follow without a long call.
- “enable caching” is straightforward
- “canonicalization” we can give examples
(2) turns into “it depends” quickly, but still valuable as an internal/SRE rec.

Caleb Johnson: re: (3) canonicalization. we need to be careful we never imply we saw their raw prompt. can we make the explanation strictly in terms of “high hash mismatch rate” + “similar request signatures” (bucketized)?

Yara Haddad: yep. current copy draft says:
“Many requests are failing prefix-cache lookup due to signature mismatch. This often happens when dynamic fields (timestamps, UUIDs, user-specific headers) are included in the cached prefix. Consider normalizing/canonicalizing those fields or moving them after the cached prefix.”
No raw prompt mention.

Nadia Rahman: also: we should ensure the “similarity proxy” feature is not per-tenant high-cardinality. if it’s computed in batch from aggregated buckets only, ok.

Mei Lin: it is. it uses prompt_len_bucket + route + model + a coarse “prompt_fingerprint_bucket” we already approved (512-way). no raw data.

Logan Wright: on (4) TTL increase being noisy — makes sense. also TTL interacts w/ memory pressure. we probably need a combined policy rec (“if TTL_expired high and eviction low, consider TTL; if eviction high, don’t”). but that’s too much for MVP.

Hassan Mirza: +1. TTL rec without modeling memory is scary.

Aditya Rao: can we decide now what goes into “ship in MVP”? i need to update PM-913.

Yara Haddad: my proposal:
SHIP (customer-facing + internal):
A) Enable caching on route (policy_disabled)
B) Prompt canonicalization (hash_mismatch) — gated on rollout completeness
SHIP (internal-only first):
C) Eviction pressure / increase max_prefix_cache_bytes (eviction_rate + wasted_prefill)
HOLD:
D) Increase TTL
E) Routing locality

Paula Mendes: i’m ok with that. B being gated is important. what’s the gate exactly?

Yara Haddad: gate for B:
- miss_reason taxonomy includes version_mismatch in all prod regions
- old metric names deprecated (post pr-18431)
- streaming retry double-counting fix fully rolled (pr-18428)
Once those are true, we can trust hash_mismatch signal.

Ethan Park: i can confirm rollout status later today. i think eu-west is still on old naming for ~20%.

Caleb Johnson: for A) we also need to ensure route allowlist works (ingestion guard). otherwise we’ll generate recs but can’t slice by route reliably.

Logan Wright: we can scope to “top N routes by traffic” where route_id is present and in allowlist. that’s consistent w/ the cardinality policy.

Miguel Santos: if C is internal-only, can we still surface it as a “support playbook” blurb? e.g. if customer asks about cost spike, we can point to eviction + wasted prefill.

Aditya Rao: yes, internal report can include all templates with a “visibility” field. MVP console stub can filter.

Nadia Rahman: please add “confidence” score to the output schema. doesn’t have to be perfect, but lets us sort + not show low-confidence.

Yara Haddad: already have a 0-1 score based on z-scores + guardrail checks. happy to share the formula if folks want to sanity check.

Hassan Mirza: can you paste formula? mostly want to ensure it’s monotonic w/ actual deltas.

Yara Haddad: rough formula (v0):
- base = sigmoid( w1*Δhit_rate + w2*wasted_prefill_pct + w3*eviction_rate ) depending on template
- then multiply by guardrail multipliers:
  - *0.0 if missing required tags
  - *0.5 if prompt_len distribution shifted
  - *0.7 if during incident failover window (from SRE annotation feed)
Weights tuned on backtest to avoid overfiring.

Logan Wright: looks good. pls make sure the incident annotation feed join isn’t flaky. we’ve had missing windows.

Mei Lin: for now it’s best-effort; if missing, we don’t downweight. (so we might overfire during failovers). for MVP, maybe safer to just suppress any “routing locality” template entirely (which we are).

Paula Mendes: decision feels clear. i’ll update PRD language: only A + B customer-facing, C internal-only.

Aditya Rao: +1. action items:
- Yara: update output schema with visibility + confidence, and add the rollout gates as feature flags
- Ethan: post rollout status for miss_reason + metric renames
- Logan/Nadia: sign off on guardrails + allowlists

Yara Haddad: ack. will push a small update to the batch job design doc + link the backtest tables. :thumbsup:

Miguel Santos: :thumbsup: once you have sample outputs, i’ll review the copy from “what would a support engineer say” angle.

Caleb Johnson: :eyes: on the privacy wording. ping me when you have final template copy, i’ll do a quick read.

Ethan Park: will reply here later w/ rollout % by region.

Nadia Rahman: thanks all. pls keep the taxonomy stable once we ship. changing miss reasons later will break the rec pipeline + dashboards.

Hassan Mirza: +100. ship stable, iterate on thresholds, not on enums.

title: #eng-platform: 1741073409
preview:
Mina Haddad: circling back on the `recommended` alias semantics. i’m still worried we’ll accidentally create “silent upgrades” if a customer sets `quantization_profile_ref=recommended` and we treat it as a dynamic pointer.

Ethan Park: +1, we need to lock this down before we finalize config service behavior. question: do we *ever* want the stored route config to be `recommended`?

Jordan Lee: if we store the alias, determinism is gone unless we define a specific resolution moment. and even then audit diffs get weird (config unchanged, behavior changed).

Benji Okafor: customer ergonomics: lots of folks will ask for “just keep me on the best profile”. but i agree we can’t do silent behavior changes for v1.

Priya Natarajan: we promised opt-in and reversible. “no customer action” changes violate that. also enterprise change control teams will block us.

Chloe Martin: from Console POV: the button label “Enable recommended” implies it follows updates. we should either change copy or make it explicit it pins.

Ravi Menon: we also have the multi-region issue: “recommended” could be different between H100 and A10 pools. if route config is just alias, policy eval has to resolve per region/gpu class, which makes it hard to reason about rollbacks.

Quentin Harris: model registry can expose `recommended_profile_id` per (model_id, variant, gpu_class, context_bucket) but i really don’t want callers depending on “recommended changes == live behavior changes”. we’ll be updating that field as we publish/rollback.

Naomi Feldman: audit/RBAC angle: if a profile changes under the hood, who is the “actor”? we’d need audit events for “system updated recommended pointer” and also a linkage to which customer routes were affected. that’s a lot for v1.

Ethan Park: proposal: API accepts `recommended` as *input only*; config service resolves it at write-time and persists the concrete `profile_id`. runtime/policy never sees alias.

Jordan Lee: +1. also store resolution metadata in the audit event (requested_ref, resolved_profile_id, resolution_scope). but route config itself stays concrete.

Benji Okafor: ok so semantics are: 
- `recommended` = “resolve now” 
- it does NOT auto-follow updates 
we should return something in the write response like `resolved_quant_profile_id` so it’s not surprising.

Chloe Martin: we can add UI copy: “Pins the current recommended version. Future recommended updates won’t apply unless you change this setting.” and maybe show the resolved id inline (with a “copy” action).

Ravi Menon: and if the pinned id becomes deprecated/blocked later, policy engine should fallback to baseline + emit `fallback_reason=deprecated_profile` and Console should surface a warning banner.

Priya Natarajan: yes. but importantly: *no automatic repointing* to the new recommended. fallback only, and customer chooses what to do next.

Quentin Harris: we can support that with registry states: `status=active|deprecated|blocked` and policy can treat `blocked` as hard fail (or forced fallback) depending on rollout mode. but i’d prefer forced fallback for safety.

Naomi Feldman: audit event should capture both the pinned id and any subsequent fallback events. esp for FalconBank-ish customers asking “prove nothing changed without approval”.

Ethan Park: sounds like consensus. action items:
- i’ll update config service doc + implementation: accept `recommended` input, persist resolved id only
- Chloe: update Console copy to “Pin recommended” or add explicit note
- Jordan/Ravi: confirm policy behavior for deprecated/blocked profile ids (fallback + metric)
- Benji: update OpenAPI + SDK notes so clients don’t assume it’s dynamic

Chloe Martin: ack, will add to DES/ENG notes. we’ll also add a tooltip “Want auto-updates? Not supported in v1” so we don’t overpromise.

Benji Okafor: i’ll also add an API error message for folks trying to PATCH with `recommended` and expecting it to stick: response includes `stored_quantization_profile_ref=<id>`.

Priya Natarajan: perfect. “no silent profile changes” is the line we should repeat in docs + sales.

Jordan Lee: i’ll append this to ADR language: “Aliases MAY be accepted at write time for convenience but MUST NOT be persisted; system will not silently change pinned profiles.”

Priya Natarajan: perfect. “no silent profile changes” is the line we should repeat in docs + sales.

Jordan Lee: i’ll append this to ADR language: “Aliases MAY be accepted at write time for convenience but MUST NOT be persisted; system will not silently change pinned profiles.”

title: #eng-platform: 1740689022
preview:
Keiko Yamamoto: heads up — propose hotfix for unfair burst denials we saw in staging when telemetry is laggy. tl;dr token bucket thinks tenants are “spending” more than they are b/c usage samples arrive late/out of order → oscillation → denial spikes.

Keiko Yamamoto: plan is telemetry smoothing + stale-sample guard:
- per-tenant `observed_gpu_seconds` becomes EWMA over last N samples (window 30s, alpha=0.2)
- clamp negative deltas when samples arrive out-of-order
- if telemetry age > 20s, admission controller falls back to conservative mode: don’t decrease tokens, only refill; and emit `DENY_TELEMETRY_STALE` once global safety cap is near
- keep determinism: smoothing uses only controller-local time buckets + monotonic seq, no random

Keiko Yamamoto: PR is in progress (will be `pr-23944-hotfix-prevent-burst-starvation-under-telemetry-lag`). want quick review from @Jordan @Allison @Peter on risk + rollout timing.

Jordan Lee: yeah the oscillation is what we saw in the sim too. couple qs:
1) for “clamp negative deltas” — are we just `max(0, sample - last_sample)`? if so, can we still overcount if we drop a late small sample and then get a larger one?
2) “don’t decrease tokens” in stale mode could accidentally *admit more* than intended. what’s the gating there?

Keiko Yamamoto: 1) we’re tracking per-tenant cumulative usage w/ `usage_seq`. if seq regresses, we ignore. if seq advances but delta < 0, clamp to 0. (we don’t accept a “smaller cumulative” value.)
2) stale mode is only when `telemetry_lag_seconds > 20` AND `fleet_burst_saturation < 0.85`. when saturation is high, we flip to deny-fast with `DENY_POOL_SAFETY` not “admit more”.

Allison Grant: from observability side: pls make sure we can *see* when stale mode is active. can we add a counter + gauge?
- `burst_admission_telemetry_stale_mode{pool,region}`
- `burst_admission_telemetry_lag_seconds{pool,region}` (we already have one but label set differs)
and pls no per-tenant labels on anything that pages us :)

Keiko Yamamoto: yup. we’ll add:
- gauge `burst_admission_telemetry_lag_seconds{pool,region,hw_class}`
- counter `burst_admission_telemetry_stale_mode_enter_total{pool,...}` + `..._exit_total`
no tenant label.

Peter Holtz: SRE paranoia: “don’t decrease tokens” when telemetry is stale sounds like it could violate neighbor protection for a few mins, esp if the pool is already tight.
can we set stale mode to be *more conservative* instead? like cap burst ratio to 1.05 until telemetry recovers.

Keiko Yamamoto: fair. current patch is: stale mode is conservative in 2 ways:
- only applies when saturation low (<0.85). if saturation is high, we deny.
- additionally reduces tenant cap multiplier from configured (e.g. 1.5x) down to 1.1x while stale.
so it should be “keep system stable” not “let it rip”.

Jordan Lee: ok that’s better. also need to ensure reason codes are stable: if we deny due to stale telemetry, should be `DENY_TELEMETRY_STALE` (not flapping between that and `DENY_TOKENS_EXHAUSTED`). helps support.

Benji Okafor: +1. i can update the mapping in the controller to prefer stale reason when in stale mode.

Ethan Park: control plane wise, no schema changes right? just runtime behavior + metrics.

Keiko Yamamoto: correct, no config changes. just a code path + metrics.

Rafael Mendes: what’s rollout plan? need to know if this goes out before tomorrow’s beta check-in. also want to avoid surprises for oncall.

Keiko Yamamoto: proposal:
- merge today, deploy to staging immediately
- canary prod: us-east / H100 burst pool only, allow-list 2 internal tenants (dogfood + loadtest)
- monitor 30-60 mins: denial rate by reason, p95 latency for non-burst tenants, and telemetry lag gauge
- if clean, expand to remaining us-east pools
- tomorrow morning expand eu-west (where we saw the worst lag)

Peter Holtz: pls tie it to feature flag + have rollback switch ready (i know we have global kill but want per-pool if possible).

Benji Okafor: we can do per-pool toggle. existing flag `burst.telemetrySmoothing.enabled` supports `{region,hw_class}` filter. will set default off.

Allison Grant: metrics/dashboard: if you’re deploying today, ping me once the metric names are final. i’ll update the dashboard panel query in `burst saturation / denials` to include stale mode.

Jordan Lee: any perf impact? EWMA per tenant on every admission call could be a lot.

Keiko Yamamoto: it’s per-pool in-memory map (tenant_id -> smoothed usage). update occurs on telemetry ingest tick (5s), not per request. admission path reads precomputed value. so negligible.

Vikram Singh: routing question: if admission returns `DENY_TELEMETRY_STALE`, gateway should treat it like “retry w/ backoff” or “fallback to baseline”? current behavior for denials is mostly immediate 429.

Jordan Lee: for now, same as other “capacity” denials: no fallback cross-tenant; just return 429 + retry-after. but making it explicit reason code helps.

Peter Holtz: + add a note in oncall: if we see stale mode entering frequently, likely telemetry pipeline degradation. so root cause might be metrics/telemetry path, not admission.

Keiko Yamamoto: agree. will add note to runbook section “Symptom: DENY_TELEMETRY_STALE spikes”.

DeployBot: Deployed build `burst-admission-controller@9f3c1b2` to staging (env: stg-usw2) (requested by Benji Okafor)

Benji Okafor: staging deploy is live. i’ll run the load scenario that previously caused denial spikes.

Benji Okafor: quick update: on stg loadtest, denials dropped from ~18% to ~2% during induced 25s telemetry lag. stale mode entered once, cap multiplier reduced to 1.1x, no p95 regression on non-burst tenant synthetic.

Allison Grant: nice. can you paste the metric names as they landed?

Benji Okafor: `burst_admission_telemetry_lag_seconds{pool,region,hw_class}` + `burst_admission_telemetry_stale_mode{pool,region,hw_class}` (0/1 gauge) + enter/exit counters.

Allison Grant: perfect. i’ll wire into dashboard tonight.

Rafael Mendes: ok for prod canary when?

Keiko Yamamoto: aiming 6pm PT canary us-east/H100. @Peter can you confirm oncall coverage? we’ll watch for 60 mins and be ready to flip per-pool flag off.

Peter Holtz: i’m on until 8pm PT. works. please drop link to the canary dashboard + the flag path.

Keiko Yamamoto: dashboard: grafana -> “Dedicated Burst / Admission + Telemetry” (will paste link in channel once Allison updates). flag path: `features/burst/telemetrySmoothing` in config service, scope `region=us-east hw=H100`.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: pls add a short comment in PR describing determinism guarantee + why EWMA is safe (no request-order dependency). that’ll help future ADR references.

Keiko Yamamoto: will do. thanks all.

title: #eng-platform: 1740518300
preview:
Benji Okafor: quick review thread: redaction rules for config diffs (bot v2 + config diff API). goal is customer/tenant-safe diffs in Slack. can we align on allowlist + tests so we don’t leak anything?

Benji Okafor: current draft rules (WIP):
- allowlist specific config paths (feature flags, routing policy, quotas, model pins)
- denylist patterns for keys (token/secret/password/key/etc)
- value redaction if looks like credential (JWT-ish, PEM, long base64)
- default redact unknown keys

Benji Okafor: tagging @Kaitlyn Nguyen @Marcus Lin @Naomi Feldman for security signoff, and @Ethan Park / @Jordan Lee / @Mina Haddad for “what’s actually useful in incident triage”.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1 on default-redact unknown keys. if we ever do “best effort detect secrets” without default-redact, we’ll lose.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: question: are we diffing *effective* config or *declared* config? effective config can include merged env/overrides -> higher chance of accidentally pulling creds.

Benji Okafor: good call. current plan is “declared control plane config only” (feature flags / routing policies / quota) not env or runtime vars. we can explicitly exclude env var sources.

Marcus Lin: strong preference: no regex-only approach. do allowlist + schema-based classification. denylist patterns are fine as belt+suspenders.

Marcus Lin: also, Slack is an untrusted sink. treat it as “public-ish internal”. don’t show anything that would be sensitive if screenshotted.

Naomi Feldman: agree. for compliance, the statement should be: “Slack diff output is safe-by-default and does not require elevated clearance.” anything requiring elevated access should be a link gated behind authz + audit.

Mina Haddad: from routing perspective, responders need to see:
- routing policy name/version
- fallback policy enabled/disabled
- regional weights + any recent changes
- pinned model + fallback model
- any load shedding / admission control toggles

Mina Haddad: they do NOT need raw upstream URLs, credentials, header injection rules, or customer identifiers.

Jordan Lee: for policy engine, useful fields are mostly booleans + enums. ex:
- `policy.fallback_mode`
- `policy.max_queue_ms`
- `policy.backpressure.enabled`
- `policy.routing.tier` / `customer_tier`

Jordan Lee: risky fields: anything “expression-like” or templates. we have some rules that include snippets. should redact full contents for those.

Ethan Park: +1. also we should prevent showing full tenant lists. sometimes policies include allow/deny sets by tenant_id.

Benji Okafor: ok so allowlist should be path-based, and for some paths show only summarized version (counts, hash).

Ravi Menon: degraded mode decisions need visibility into *what knobs are set* not the full objects. ex: show “fallback to X region enabled” and “min health threshold = 3/5” rather than full JSON.

Devon Price: from Slack UX side: if we redact heavily, we should be explicit in the card like “(redacted)” + “open in Console (audited)” link.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: can we standardize the redaction marker? ex `«REDACTED»` for values and `«REDACTED_KEY»` for keys? helps with automated tests to ensure we didn’t forget.

Benji Okafor: yep. we can also include a “redaction_reason” internally but not show in Slack.

Marcus Lin: careful: don’t include reasons like “matched pattern aws_secret_access_key” in Slack either. keep it simple.

Benji Okafor: proposed allowlist (initial):
```
feature_flags.*
routing.policy_name
routing.policy_version
routing.fallback.enabled
routing.fallback.mode
routing.fallback.regions
routing.weights.*
quota.requests_per_min
quota.tokens_per_min
rate_limit.*  (but only numeric thresholds)
models.primary
models.fallback
models.pin_reason
rollout.phase
rollout.canary.enabled
rollout.canary.percent
```

Benji Okafor: proposed “summary-only” list (show counts + stable hash, not raw contents):
```
routing.tenant_allowlist
routing.tenant_denylist
policy.expressions
policy.templates
```

Ethan Park: looks good. on `rate_limit.*` I’d be specific: allow numeric fields only. sometimes we store “keyed-by” details.

Jordan Lee: +1. we have `rate_limit.key` and `rate_limit.dimensions` -> should redact.

Mina Haddad: also `routing.weights.*` might include region ids only. that’s fine. but if it includes “endpoint” strings, redact.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: tests:
1) unit tests with known-bad keys (`password`, `secret`, `token`, `api_key`, `private_key`) anywhere in path -> always redacted
2) value tests: detect JWT, PEM blocks, long base64, long hex
3) snapshot/golden test of a representative diff payload -> ensure output contains only allowed paths
4) property-based-ish: generate random keys/paths and assert default redacts unless allowlisted

Marcus Lin: +1 and add “negative assertion” tests: if a test config includes a sentinel secret value (e.g. `SUPER_SECRET_DO_NOT_LEAK_123`) it must never appear in output. easy grep.

Naomi Feldman: also ensure audit logging for the underlying fetch is in place, but that’s separate from Slack redaction. Slack can’t be the only control.

Kevin Osei: on audit: if bot hits the config diff endpoint, we log `incident_bot.config_diff.read` with actor, incident id, tenant scope, and whether break-glass was used.

Kevin Osei: redaction happens *before* Slack render, right? (ideally in the config service or diff service so nobody downstream can accidentally log it)

Benji Okafor: yes. plan is redact in the diff service response (shared lib used by config service + incident-bot). Slack renderer only sees redacted fields.

Devon Price: please also ensure we don’t accidentally log the raw diff at debug level in bot. we’ve been bitten by that.

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: we’ll add “no raw diff logs” lint/grep in the bot repo too.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: re: break-glass: even with break-glass, Slack should still be redacted. elevated access should mean “can click through to Console view” not “unredacted posted in thread”.

Ethan Park: +1. if someone needs unredacted, they can query in a secured tool, not Slack.

Mina Haddad: can we include a “diff confidence” line? like “partial diff (some fields unavailable)” so responders don’t assume it’s complete.

Benji Okafor: yep. we’ll add flags like `diff_status=complete|partial|unavailable` + error summary (non-sensitive).

Marcus Lin: final ask: document this as an explicit standard: allowlist paths + default redact + tests. and require security review for allowlist expansion.

Benji Okafor: SGTM. action items:
- Benji: update allowlist to be explicit on rate_limit numeric-only + summary-only paths
- Kaitlyn: add sentinel secret tests + jwt/pem/base64 detection tests
- Devon: add bot-side “no raw diff logs” guard
- Marcus/Naomi: quick async signoff once PR is up

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: please add a short note in the card: “Safe view. Some fields intentionally redacted.”

Kevin Osei: and ensure audit event names are stable for SIEM dashboards.

Benji Okafor: cool. I’ll post PR link here when up.

title: #eng-platform: 1740323666
preview:
Connor O'Brien: we need to get ahead of rollout dashboards for RBAC v2 flags before we start canarying in prod. can we align on *what* we’re measuring + who owns panels/alerts?

Connor O'Brien: main flags in play (as of current branch):
- `rbac_v2.dual_read` (evaluate v1 + v2, enforce v1, emit compare metrics)
- `rbac_v2.enforce_gateway`
- `rbac_v2.enforce_services`
- `rbac_v2.debug_denial_headers` (admin-only)

Connor O'Brien: goals for dashboards: deny rate, latency impact, mismatch rate (v1 vs v2), and “coverage gaps” (unannotated endpoints / unknown permissions). need views per env (staging/prod) + per deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private).

Logan Wright: +1. I can own the metrics plumbing / grafana dashboard skeleton. need names/labels nailed down so we don’t end up with 12 almost-identical queries.

Allison Grant: for alerts, we should keep it minimal but high-signal. deny spikes + authz eval latency + errors in evaluator client.

Amara Diallo: gateway side: we already emit `http_requests_total{status}` etc but authz needs its own to avoid mixing legitimate 403s with other 403s.

Amara Diallo: proposing gateway metrics (prom):
- `authz_check_total{component="gateway", decision="allow|deny", reason_code, permission, scope_type, env}`
- `authz_check_latency_ms_bucket{component="gateway", env}`
- `authz_middleware_fail_open_total{component="gateway", cause}` (should be 0; but if we ever do safe fallback we need a counter)

Jordan Lee: on mismatch rate in dual_read: we should emit *both* the raw decision and the diff.

Jordan Lee: ex:
- `authz_decision_total{mode="v1|v2", decision="allow|deny", component, env}`
- `authz_decision_mismatch_total{component, env, mismatch_type="v1_allow_v2_deny|v1_deny_v2_allow"}`

Jordan Lee: mismatch_type is critical for rollout. v1_deny_v2_allow is “probably ok” (v2 less strict) but v1_allow_v2_deny is the scary one.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: for services, can we include `service` label? otherwise it’s hard to isolate Control Plane vs console backend etc.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also need a way to track “missing annotation” vs “explicit deny”. we’ve seen both show up as 403 at the edge.

Ethan Park: control plane can emit:
- `authz_enforcement_total{service="control-plane", outcome="allowed|denied|error", denial_reason="policy|missing_permission_annotation|invalid_scope"}`
- `authz_annotation_missing_total{service, route}` (this is the one i want a CI gate on eventually)

Leila Farouk: for rollout criteria, can we define thresholds now? otherwise people will stare at dashboards w/out knowing what “good” looks like.

Connor O'Brien: yes pls. needs to be simple + written into rollout runbook. thinking:
- p95 gateway added latency from authz < 5ms (p99 < 15ms)
- mismatch (v1_allow_v2_deny) < 0.05% of authz checks in canary slice
- error rate in evaluator client < 0.01%
- deny rate: we track but not “must be low” globally, since denies are workload-dependent. what we care about is *delta* post-enable.

Allison Grant: +1 to delta-based deny. alert on “deny rate increased by X%” over baseline per route or per permission.

Allison Grant: but we need a baseline window. recommend:
- compare last 10m vs 1h rolling avg (or same time previous day) for canary cohort.

Logan Wright: cohorting is the hard part. do we have a label for canary vs non-canary? if not, we can do it by `flag_state` label on metrics.

Jordan Lee: we can add `flag_state="dual_read|enforce"` label when emitting metrics from middleware/library. it’ll help a lot.

Amara Diallo: gateway can do that. basically tag metrics with `rbac_mode` based on which flag branch executed.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: + need `policy_version` label? at least for audit logs, but for metrics too it’s useful to correlate rollout with policy bundle version.

Jordan Lee: careful w/ cardinality. `policy_version` as a label can explode if it’s a git sha. maybe we do a limited `policy_release="2025-02-23.1"` style.

Logan Wright: agree. let’s use `policy_release` (semver-like) not sha.

Priya Natarajan: stepping in: whatever we ship, keep metrics cardinality sane. do NOT label on `user_id` / `org_id`. permission label is already a lot.

Priya Natarajan: can we sample permission label? e.g., keep per-permission in logs, but metrics roll up permission to `permission_family` (like `controlplane.roles.*`).

Amara Diallo: on gateway, permission strings are high-cardinality but bounded-ish. still, we have ~200-400 perms now and growing. might be ok but we should sanity check.

Allison Grant: bounded perms are fine. it’s `route` that gets messy. suggest:
- metric labels: `permission`, `decision`, `reason_code`, `component`, `service`, `env`, `rbac_mode`
- keep `route` only on a separate metric used for coverage/annotation missing (and we gate on it)

Ethan Park: works for me. for “missing annotation” we can label on `route_template` (e.g., `/v1/orgs/:orgId/roles`) not full path.

Leila Farouk: can we add an explicit panel for “top denial reason codes”? support will ask for this day 1.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yes, and “top permissions by deny volume” too. plus “top permissions by mismatch volume” during dual_read.

Connor O'Brien: ok dashboard sections i’m hearing:
1) Rollout Overview (by env): flag adoption (dual_read/enforce), total checks, allow/deny, errors
2) Safety: mismatch counts/rate (highlight v1_allow_v2_deny)
3) Performance: authz latency p50/p95/p99, cache hit rate
4) Coverage: missing annotation, unknown permission registry errors
5) Denials: top reason codes, top permissions by denies

Logan Wright: cache metrics from evaluator client:
- `authz_cache_hit_total{cache="decision", component, env}`
- `authz_cache_miss_total{...}`
- `authz_cache_stale_total{...}` (if we detect invalidation race)

Jordan Lee: + `authz_policy_fetch_latency_ms` and `authz_policy_fetch_errors_total` (if client calls policy service / bundle fetch)

Amara Diallo: where are we putting dashboards? Grafana folder `Platform / AuthZ`?

Allison Grant: `Security / RBAC v2 Rollout` might be more discoverable for SecOps + GRC too.

Priya Natarajan: +1 Security folder, but Platform needs edit rights.

Logan Wright: I’ll create `Security/RBAC v2` folder and add:
- `RBAC v2 - Rollout Overview`
- `RBAC v2 - Dual Read Comparison`
- `RBAC v2 - Performance & Caching`

Connor O'Brien: alerts proposal (please poke holes):
- ALERT A: `authz_decision_mismatch_total{mismatch_type="v1_allow_v2_deny"}` rate > 0.1% for 5m in prod canary
- ALERT B: `authz_check_latency_ms` p99 > 20ms for 10m
- ALERT C: `authz_enforcement_total{outcome="error"}` > 0.01% of checks
- ALERT D: `authz_annotation_missing_total` > 0 (in prod) after we flip enforce_services

Ethan Park: ALERT D should be “> 0 in last 10m” once enforce is on. before that it’ll be noisy while we annotate.

Amara Diallo: for gateway latency, separate out “middleware time” from total request latency if possible. otherwise the alert will fire due to unrelated downstream slowness.

Logan Wright: we already measure middleware phase timing in gateway. i’ll plumb it into `authz_check_latency_ms` directly.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: what about “deny rate jump” alert? ex customer gets locked out due to mapping bug.

Allison Grant: I’d avoid global deny rate alert, but we can do “new deny reason codes” / “denies due to missing binding” spiking.

Jordan Lee: we have reason codes from the new deny struct. suggestion:
- alert on `reason_code="NO_BINDING"` and `reason_code="SCOPE_MISMATCH"` spikes specifically.

Connor O'Brien: ok. so alert E:
- `authz_check_total{decision="deny", reason_code=~"NO_BINDING|SCOPE_MISMATCH"}` increase > 3x baseline (15m vs 2h) in prod canary.

Leila Farouk: rollout stages — can we put them in the dashboard as an annotation? like vertical lines when we flipped flags.

Logan Wright: yes. if we emit a deploy event / flag flip event to Grafana annotations. can be manual but better if automated.

DeployBot: Deployment started: `control-plane@rbac-v2-metrics-annot` to environment `staging` (initiated by Logan Wright)

Amara Diallo: staged enablement plan reminder (so dashboard filters match):
- staging: dual_read on 100%
- prod: dual_read 5% canary for 24h -> 25% -> 100%
- then enforce_gateway 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100%
- then enforce_services behind service-specific allowlist (control-plane first)

Ethan Park: for Dedicated/Private: we should default to dual_read only until policy bundle has the dedicated/private role templates shipped. otherwise we’ll see NO_BINDING storms.

Connor O'Brien: yep. i’ll add “deployment_mode” filter. dedicated/private will start later with pilot accounts.

Priya Natarajan: action items:
- Logan: create Grafana dashboards + wire required metrics names/labels (today/tomorrow)
- Amara: ensure gateway emits rbac_mode + reason_code labels + middleware latency (this week)
- Ethan/Kaitlyn: service-side missing annotation counters + reason_code parity (this week)
- Connor: update rollout runbook w/ thresholds + alerting + stage plan (by EOW)

Logan Wright: works. I’ll post links once dashboards are up.

Logan Wright: one more: do we want a “403 breakdown panel” per route? support tends to ask “is this authz or something else”.

Allison Grant: maybe a small panel: `http_403_total` split by `error_type` where `error_type="authz_denied"` vs `error_type="other"`. if we can tag responses.

Amara Diallo: gateway already sets `x-redwood-error-code`. we can map it in metrics as `error_code` (bounded). i’ll add `error_family` = `authz` for the new RBAC denies.

Connor O'Brien: perfect. please make sure debug header is only for admin + when `rbac_v2.debug_denial_headers` flag is on. we don’t want to leak internals.

Jordan Lee: agree. debug headers should include reason_code + required permission + evaluated scope, but only in admin mode.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: “why denied” UX depends on those reason codes. let’s keep the list stable.

Logan Wright: ok logging note: for audit/evidence, metrics aren’t enough. but for rollout, dashboards + alerts should keep us safe.

Logan Wright: dashboards created (staging data only for now):
- Grafana: Security/RBAC v2/Rollout Overview
- Grafana: Security/RBAC v2/Dual Read Comparison
- Grafana: Security/RBAC v2/Performance & Caching

Logan Wright: TODO: I still need `rbac_mode` label from gateway + `authz_decision_mismatch_total` from policy lib to light up the comparison panels.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: thanks. once those land, we can start using this as the “go/no-go” page for canary flips.

Leila Farouk: once you’ve got the mismatch panel live, ping me. i’ll wire an automated smoke that hits key endpoints and we can watch mismatch/denies on staging before prod.

Allison Grant: also tagging SecOps once alerts are in place. we should have an oncall playbook entry: “if mismatch spike, rollback to dual_read / enforce off” w/ exact flag commands.

Connor O'Brien: yep, i’ll put the exact `rbacctl` commands in runbook + link dashboards.
